coredata.dc:title: Amino-functional carbon quantum dots as a rational nanosensor for Cu2+
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Saipeng
Wang, Wenxian
Cheng, Jiewei
Zhou, Xin
Xie, Mengyao
Luo, Qing
Yang, Dan
Zhou, Yufei
Wen, Huiyun
Xue, Weiming
coredata.dc:description: Intracellular copper (Cu2+) plays a crucial role in regulation of physiological and biological processes. Nevertheless, excessive copper from bioaccumulation may cause potential risk to human body. Hence, effective and sensitive identification system is essential to avoid excessive intake of copper. A novel highly sensitive fluorescent probe has been designed for detecting copper ion. Carbon dots was synthesized by citric acid and poly(amido amine) dendrimers (2G-PAMAM) as carbon and nitrogen source, respectively. Amino-functionalized carbon dots (NH2-CDs) was obtained by further modification by methyl acrylate and ethylenediamine reaction, which illustrated high quantum yield of 20% and small particle size of 25 nm. The functionalized NH2-CDs offered a rapid, sensitive and selective recognition of Cu2+ with a detection limit of 5 nM and a dynamic linear range from 0.005 to 600 μM. As for practical application, the nanosensor illustrated good cell permeability and low cytotoxicity, and thus successfully applied for fluorescence imaging and biosensing of trace Cu2+ in MCF-7 and HepG-2 cells, which suggested the potential biological application in disease diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Copper ions
Fluorescent probe
Nanosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and potential interferences for Cu2+ detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Opening Foundation of Key Laboratory of Resource Biology and Biotechnology in Western China
Shaanxi Provincial Natural Science Basic Research Project

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Foundation of Shaanxi Provincial Education Department

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from Foundation of Shaanxi Provincial Education Department ( 16JK1770 ) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation on the 61st Project ( 2017M613190 ). Shaanxi Provincial Natural Science Basic Research Project, China ( 2018JM2037 ) and Opening Foundation of Key Laboratory of Resource Biology and Biotechnology in Western China (Northwest University), Ministry of Education ( ZSK2018008 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of NH2-CDs (A) TEM image and size distribution of bare-CDs. (B) TEM image and size distribution of NH2-CDs. (C) FTIR spectra obtained for NH2-CDs (black line), NH2-CDs + Cu2+ (red line) and bare-CDs (blue line). (D) UV absorption spectra (a, b), fluorescence excitation spectra (c, d) and fluorescence emission spectra of NH2-CDs and NH2-CDs + Cu2+ (e, f). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
XPS spectra of the NH2-CDs. (A) XPS full scan, (B) C1s, (C) N1s, (D) O1s.
Fluorescence character of NH2-CDs. (A) Fluorescence spectra of NH2-CDs excited at different excitation wavelength. (B) Concentration-dependent fluorescence response of NH2-CDs.

Fluorescence response of NH2-CDs. (A) Fluorescence spectra change of 50 μg·mL−1 NH2-CDs upon addition of various concentrations of copper ions; (B) A linear relationship of the fluorescence intensity versus the logarithm concentration of copper ions with the range from 0.005 to 600 μM (λex = 320 nm). (C) Fluorescence quench and restored by EDTA. (D) The effect of EDTA concentration on fluorescence of NH2-CDs.

The selectivity of NH2-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) in different condition. (A) Copper ions over other ions (10 μM); (B) The selectivity of bare CDs for copper ions (10 μM); (C) Different amino acids (10 μM); (D) Different pH.
The photostability of 50 μg/mL CDs in different condition. (A) buffer influences; (B) NaCl with different concentrations; (C) Radiation time; (D) Storage time.
Cellular cytotoxicity assessment of (A) HepG-2 and (B) MCF-7 after incubation with NH2-CDs.
Confocal images of HepG-2 and MCF-7 incubated with NH2-CD ssensor and treated with copper ions.
Confocal images of HepG-2 and MCF-7 cells incubated with NH2-CDs sensor and copper ions at different time.
Dynamic fluorescence images in Z axis of MCF-7 cells incubated with NH2-CDs.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of bare-CDs and NH2-CDs.
The schematic diagram for fluorescence quenching of NH2-CDs by copper ions.
Comparison between NH2-CDs and of various reported carbon dots for copper ions detection.

head.ce:title: Amino-functional carbon quantum dots as a rational nanosensor for Cu2+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Intracellular copper (Cu2+) plays a crucial role in regulation of physiological and biological processes. Nevertheless, excessive copper from bioaccumulation may cause potential risk to human body. Hence, effective and sensitive identification system is essential to avoid excessive intake of copper. A novel highly sensitive fluorescent probe has been designed for detecting copper ion. Carbon dots was synthesized by citric acid and poly(amido amine) dendrimers (2G-PAMAM) as carbon and nitrogen source, respectively. Amino-functionalized carbon dots (NH2-CDs) was obtained by further modification by methyl acrylate and ethylenediamine reaction, which illustrated high quantum yield of 20% and small particle size of 25 nm. The functionalized NH2-CDs offered a rapid, sensitive and selective recognition of Cu2+ with a detection limit of 5 nM and a dynamic linear range from 0.005 to 600 μM. As for practical application, the nanosensor illustrated good cell permeability and low cytotoxicity, and thus successfully applied for fluorescence imaging and biosensing of trace Cu2+ in MCF-7 and HepG-2 cells, which suggested the potential biological application in disease diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Intracellular copper ions (Cu2+) play a significant role in the electron transfer of physiological and biological regulation [1]. Importantly, copper is a cofactor for numerous enzymes and quite indispensable to the electron transfer processes of various biological reactions in the human body [2,3]. Nevertheless, inadequate copper can affect enzyme activity and caused various neurological disease in human body, such as Wilson and Alzheimer’s disease [4]. Meanwhile, excessive copper ion is highly toxicant and harmful to human health, such as seriously damage the kidneys and other organs of the human body [5,6]. Hence, the development of effective strategies for monitoring copper ions in living cells can promote to comprehend their complex contributions to healthy and pathological states, which is of great importance to provide effective early diagnosis and prevention of disease.

According to the U.S Environmental Protection Agency, the upper limitation in drinking water is 20 μM [7]. Therefore, a high sensitive and selective method for monitor copper ion concentration is extremely demanded. In recent decades, various analytical techniques have been applied to recognize copper ion, including electrochemical sensors [8], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [9], ion chromatography [10], potentiometric sensors [11,12], atomic fluorescence spectrometry and atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [13,14]. However, these techniques are still limited because of their high cost and complicated sample preparation. Compared with these traditional detection technologies, biological imaging with selective and sensitive florescence sensors offered an attractive approach to monitor intracellular copper ions [15,16]. Considerable efforts have been devoted to develop fluorescence sensor including biomolecules or organic molecules [17–20], inorganic nanomaterials [21], as exemplified by carbon nanotubes [22], semiconductor nanostructures or gold nanoparticles [23,24]. However, it is limited because of the cumbersome preparation process and high biotoxicity to organism.

Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a novel water-soluble and highly biocompatible fluorescent nanosenor for recognizing intracellular copper ion. Carbon dots (CDs), as novel fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, have attracted widespread attention because of their unique electronic and optical properties, including non-blinking, high water solubility, surface functionalities, strong photostability and favorable biocompatibility [25–29]. Recently, considerable excellent research about CDs has been reported for precise identification of trace copper ions based on their special fluorescence properties (Table 1 ). Tian also developed ratiometric fluorescent carbon-dot-based dual-emission nanohybrid produces for the measurement of cellular copper ions [30]. Dong designed selective and sensitive fluorescent carbon quantum dots for Cu2+, which functionalized with polyamine and applied to detect copper ions in river water sample [31]. Shangguan and his co-workers developed a dual-emission fluorescent nanoprobe toward Cu2+ detection based on the surface functionalities of Rhodamine B-doped silica nanoparticles, which was successfully utilized in HeLa cells for copper detection and fluorescence imaging [32]. Liu firstly designed a blue CDs for Cu2+ from natural plants via hydrothermal treatment, which exhibited a wide detection range [33]. Zou designed and prepared a core-satellite hybrid nanosensor by a carbodiimide-mediated approach, which utilized silica microspheres to enhance the brightness and stability of fluorescence signal [34]. Zong designed CDs for highly sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ and L-Cys by “off–on” approach [35].

Although CDs utilized in trace copper ions detection have acquired some progress and achievements (Table 1), limitations and deficiencies exemplified by high background interference and biological toxicity, low selectivity and sensitivity, which still existed and restricted further application in biological systems. Hence, we investigated the interactions mechanism between functionalized CDs and Cu2+ ions in detail. The surface amino groups of carbon dots was the key point to bind Cu2+, which lead to selectively and quickly florescent quench of CDs (Fig. 1 ). On this basis, to further broaden their applications in copper ions recognition field, a rapid identification with more additional amino groups has been developed for nanomolar detection of intracellular trace copper ions. Ethylenediamine as a functioned group was firstly grafted on the surface of CDs through methyl acrylate. The amino-functional CDs illustrated a high selectivity, sensitivity and fast response to Cu2+ and has been successfully applied to detect trace Cu2+ in MCF-7 and HepG-2 cells, which suggested a promising applications in early diagnosis and treatment of disease.

All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and utilized as received without further purification. Doubly distilled water was utilized for the preparation of all aqueous solutions. Hydrochloric (37.5, wt.%), citric acid (CA, 99.5%), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98.5%), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 99.0%) were all purchased from Sigma. 2.0G-PAMAM, amino acids, Glutathione (GSH) and all inorganic reagents were received from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Regent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), dialysis membranes (MW: 1000) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals Co.

UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-T6 spectrophotometer (shangyi, China). Fluorescence emission spectra were measured on a flame fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) measurements were carried out on a Frontier spectrometer in transmission mode with a KBr window. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a JEM-2100F microscope (JEOL, Japan). Freeze-drying in vacuum (FD-I-50), circumrotation evaporation (RE-52A) and high speed centrifuge (3H20RI) were utilized for the preparation and purification of carbon dots.

The bare-CDs was synthesized from citric acid and 2.0 generation dendritic polyamide-amine (2.0G-PAMAM) by persistenthightemperatureat 150 °C, which was purified by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm/min for 10 min and the supernatant was filtrated by syringe filters (0.22 μm), which was dried under vacuum freeze-dried. The dry bare-CDs stored in dryer for further modification.

8 mL methyl acrylate was added dropwise to bare-CDs in methanol and stirred for 48 h in room temperature, then the reaction solution was evaporated by vacuum rotary evaporator to remove methanol and residual methyl acrylate. Afterwards, the concentrate was dispersed by 25 mL methanol and 16 mL ethylenediamine was added dropwise and stirred for 48 h in room temperature. Next, the amino-functionalized CDs (NH2-CDs) was obtained after the residual ethylenediamine and methanol were removed by vacuum rotary evaporation. Then the products were purified by dialysis through a porous cellulose membrane (MWCO 1000 Da Sigma, Germany) against the pure water for 48 h to remove the remaining ethylenediamine and intermediate fragments. After filtration by 0.22 μm membrane, the dry NH2-CDs was collected under vacuum freeze-dried and stored in dryer for further characterization and application (Scheme 1 ).

All the fluorescence detection was performed in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 10 mM, pH 7.4). In the assay, 50 μL NH2-CDs solution (50 μg·mL−1) was dispersed into PBS buffer, Copper ions in different concentrations gradient from 0.01 μM to 30.0 μM was directly added to nanosensor solution incubating for 5 min at 37 °C, respectively. The detection was conducted by Hitachi F-4600 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer in a quartz cuvette with the excitation wavelength (λex) of 370 nm and emission wavelength (λem) of 435 nm. Considering that other metal ions and amino acids in biological system may interfere with the detection of Cu2+, the other metal ions (Co2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Cs2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Hg2+ and Al3+) and some common amino acids (Pro, ALa, Lys, Met, Ser, GLy, GLu, Leu, Val, Hyp, His, Cys and GSH) were prepared to confirm the specificity of NH2-CDs.

The cytotoxicity of NH2-CDs to MCF-7 and HePG-2 cells was examined by MTT assay. Firstly, MCF-7 and HePG-2 cells were seeded in culture flasks and grown in DMEM with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, Hyclone) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin at 37 °C in 5% CO2 atmosphere for 24 h. Then, MCF-7 and HePG-2 cells were seeded in 96-well plates (1 × 104 cells per well) with six replicates and incubated overnight in 100 μL complete medium, respectively. Then, MCF-7 and HePG-2 cells were treated with NH2-CDs in different concentration NH2-CDs (0 μg·mL−1, 2 μg·mL−1, 4 μg·mL−1, 8 μg·mL−1, 10 μg·mL−1, 20 μg·mL−1, 40 μg·mL−1, 60 μg·mL−1, 80 μg·mL−1 and 100 μg·mL−1) at 37 ◦C for 24 h. Then, 100 μL new medium with MTT (10 μL, 5 mg·mL−1) was added and incubated for 4 h to form formazan dye. The absorbance optical density at 490 nm was measured on enzyme-linked immune absorbent detector after 100 μL DMSO was added to dissolve formazan dye. The cytotoxicity of NH2-CDs was calculated by the statistical analysis of absorbance values.

The MCF-7 and HepG-2 cells were cultured in laser confocal culture dish with1.0 mL of fresh culture medium for 24 h. Then, the medium was removed and the cells were washed two times, 1.5 mL complete medium with 50 μg·mL−1 NH2-CDs was added into confocal dish for 2 h, MCF-7 and HepG-2 cells were then washed three times by phosphate buffer (PBS) to remove the residual NH2-CDs. Subsequently, different concentrations copper ions with complete medium was added into confocal dish for 30 min to detect the fluorescence changes of cells. After removing copper ions and washing three times with PBS, the cells were attached to the dish and observed by Olympus FV1000-IX81 laser confocal microscope. Blue emission from NH2-CDs was excited at 405 nm.

TEM image (Fig. 1A and B) illustrated that the bare-CDs was dispersed with an average size of around 4 nm, while it increased to 25 nm after modification with ethylenediamine on the surface. Compared to the bare-CDs, Zeta potential of NH2-CDs changed from 40.1 mV to 94.4 mV after modification by large ethylenediamine.

The infrared (IR) spectra of the bare-CDs and NH2-CDs are given in Fig. 1C, the bands at around 3280 cm−1 of bare-CDs and 3071 cm−1 of NH2-CDs are attributed to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl and amino. Moreover, the band at around 1200–1000 cm−1 indicates the existence of carbonyl (CO) group, which may be formed by thermal decomposition of citric acid. Whereas, the bands at around 1556 cm−1 corresponds to the CO stretching mode of amide bonds. More importantly, the bands at around 1561 and 1712 cm−1 are due to amide bond compared with the bare-CDs, which demonstrated that the methyl acrylate as a connecting molecule was introduced to the surface of bare-CDs (Fig. 1C). Meanwhile, the infrared (IR) spectra of NH2-CDs before and after adding copper ions were compared to explore its detection mechanism, the bands at around 3272, 2931 and 1247 cm−1 of NH2-CDs after adding copper ions illustrated very weak migration, most absorption peak hardly generated any changes (Fig. 1C). The infrared spectra further demonstrated Cu2+ are wrapped and bonded by the chelation of amino groups to form dendritic cupric amine, which promoted an effective non-radiative energy and electron transfer, resulting in quenching of NH2-CDs (Scheme 2 ).

Optical character of NH2-CDs is given in Fig. 1D (a, b) illustrates that UV absorption spectra of NH2-CDs centered at 201 nm while it gave 30 nm red shift after addition of Cu2+, which demonstrated Cu2+ was captured successfully by amine groups of NH2-CDs. Fig. 1D (c, d) and (e, f) exhibited the excitation and emission spectrum of NH2-CDs in the absence (green) and presence (blue) of 100 μM Cu2+, which illustrated that the excitation and emission wavelength of NH2-CDs was 360 nm and 455 nm, respectively. The corresponding fluorescence intensity drastically decreased after the injection of Cu2+, while the fluorescence excitation and emission peak position kept constant, which demonstrated the NH2-CDs obtained a potential to realize copper ion detection by fluorescence quench.

Meanwhile, The cuvettes illustrated 50 μg·mL−1 NH2-CDs solutions in the absence (left) and presence (right) of 100 μM Cu2+ under 365 nm UV light, the left cuvette illustrated strong blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 1D), which was consistent with the emission peak at 455 nm. However, it attenuated and quenched immediately after Cu2+ was added into the solution, which indicated that copper ion detection could be simply visualized by naked eye based on fluorescence response.

Bulk element analysis was employed to confirm the composition of the NH2-CDs The results of the bulk analyses indicated that the N-S-CDs contained N 12.5, O 35.5, C43.5, and H 8.5 wt%. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to investigate the surface states and element chemical environments of NH2-CDs. The full scan XPS spectrum illustrated that the NH2-CDs predominately consisted of C and O elements with minor N elements (Fig. 2 A). The high resolution C 1s spectrum was divided into four peaks, corresponding to CC/C–C, C–N, C–O, and –CONH- at 284.7, 285.3, 286.3, and 288.4 eV, respectively (Fig. 2B) [36]. The N 1s XPS spectrum was fitted into three peaks at 398.6 eV, 399.6 eV and 400.4 eV, which were attributed to secondary amide (OC–NH), tertiary amine (C3–N) and primary amine (C–NH2), respectively (Fig. 2C) [37]. The high-resolved O 1s XPS spectrum displayed a strong peak at 533.1 eV, which represented O 1s states in CO bonds (Fig. 2D) [38]. The C1s, N 1s, and O 1s XPS spectra clearly demonstrated the formation of NH2-CDs and different types of amines N on the surface of NH2-CDs, in accord with the FTIR analyses. The surface states both greatly contributed to the chelation of Copper ion and fluorescence reduction of NH2-CDs. Meanwhile, the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of Citric acid were very possibly existed on the surface of the NH2-CDs.

The emission wavelength of NH2-CDs kept constant at 455 nm while it was excited at wavelengths in the range of 320–420 nm. The NH2-CDs illustrated strong blue emission especially in 340–360 nm (Fig. 3 A), which demonstrated that the prepared NH2-CDs possessed stable optical properties. Therefore, the excitation wavelength was confirmed at 360 nm.

As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence of NH2-CDs improved accompanied by increased concentration of NH2-CDs, which illustrated a quite linear relationship to the concentration of NH2-CDs. Finally, 10 μg·mL−1 was eventually chosen for the detection of Cu2+, which demonstrated it the widest linear response and the lowest detection limit.

The sensitive and reliable response of NH2-CDs to Cu2+ has been investigated by a series fluorescence quenching experiments. The fluorescence response signal of NH2-CDs against the Cu2+ concentration (from top to bottom, 0, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 10, 50, 100, 250, 400 and 600 μM, respectively.) was shown in Fig. 4 A. The fluorescence intensity of NH2-CDs gradually decreased and quenched with the increasing concentration of Cu2+ and reached a plateau when the concentration of Cu2+ was higher than 200 μM, a good correlation linearity between the fluorescence intensity and the logarithm concentration of Cu2+ was obtained in the range of 0.005 to 600 μM with an excellent linear correlation of R2 = 0.99602 (Fig. 4B). The error bar represents the standard deviation of three measurements. It can be known that the fluorescence intensity is rather sensitive and high linearrelationship to the concentration of Cu2+ (Fig. 4B). The linear relationship could be fitted into F − F0 = −6.72171 lg [Cu2+] + 104.64022, corresponding to the Stern-Volmer equation: F − F0 = 1 + KSV [Trp], where F and F0 were the fluorescence intensity of the NH2-CDs at 455 nm in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively; and KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (−6.72171). The logarithm concentration of Cu2+ was considered and selected as abscissa, which attributed to trace copper ions and much lower concentration. The logarithm concentration is convenient for statistics and analysis.

What’s more important, the fluorescence intensity illustrated a large change and high sensitivity to Cu2+ even at a very low concentration of NH2-CDs (10 μg·mL−1), which gave ignorable signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio at such a low concentration. The fluorescence intensity of NH2-CDs is rather sensitive to the change of copper ions concentration and the detection limit was calculated to be 5 nM based on the ratio of signal-to noise of NH2-CDs, which is much lower than the maximum per mitted concentration (1.3 ppm, about 20 μM) of Cu2+ in drinking water confirmed by the U.S. Environment Protection Agency. Compared with single without amino-group, the formation of cupric amine complexes at the surface of NH2-CDs is the major determinant that resulted in fluorescence quenching.

Meanwhile, the control experiment between the bare CDs and NH2-CDs with the addition of copper ions have been completed and exhibited in Fig. 4C. The fluorescence of bare CDs reduced to half of the origin even at a high concentration of copper ions (400 μM), while attributed to limited amino groups on the surface of bare CDs. The fluorescence of NH2-CDs drastically reduced to one tenth of the origin, illustrating much higher sensitivity than the bare CDs (Fig. 4C), which attributed to much more amino groups were modified on the surface of NH2-CDs. The experiment result demonstrated amino groups played an important role in copper ion binding and chelation.

However, the quenched fluorescence of the NH2-CDs by Cu2+ can be obviously recovered after the addition of ethylenediamine tetraacetate (i.e. EDTA) which generally applied as metal ions chelating agent. The fluorescence restoration spectra of 10 μg·mL−1 NH2-CDs (black line), 10 μg·mL−1 NH2-CDs quenched by 400 µM Cu2+ ions (green line), 10 μg·mL−1 CDs quenched by 400 μM Cu2+ ions and recovered by 400 µM EDTA (red line) was showed by Fig. 4C. The effect of EDTA concentration and the fluorescence of NH2-CDs have been investigated and analyzed. As shown in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence of NH2-CDs quenched by 400 µM Cu2+ gradually recovered and restored with the increasing addition of EDTA, which exhibited a good correlation linearity between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of EDTA in a wide range (from 1 to 400 μM) with a correlation coefficient of 0.9934 (Fig. 4D). The linear relationship could be fitted into F − F0 = 1.52788 lg [EDTA] + 88.24794, corresponding to the Stern-Volmer equation: F − F0 = 1 + KSV [EDTA], where F and F0 were the fluorescence intensity of the NH2-CDs at 455 nm in the presence and absence and of EDTA, respectively; and KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (1.52788). The fluorescence recovered experiment illustrated that cupric amino located on the surface of NH2-CDs can be eliminated by a competitive composite reaction and thus the quenching of Cu2+ is reversible, which demonstrated the formation of cupric amine complexes was determining factor for fluorescence quenching.

The quantum yield (QY) of NH2-CDs in aqueous media was determined to be 20% by the comparative method of Williams utilized quinoline sulfate as the standard [39], which demonstrated that NH2-CDs exhibited bright green florescence and could potentially be utilized as highly-sensitive nanosensors for copper ions detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and potential interferences for Cu2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the NH2-CDs was evaluated and studied under the same conditions (Fig. 5 A). The fluorescence intensity of NH2-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) were investigated in the presence of other metal ions, including Co2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Cs2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Hg2+and Al3+ (10 μM, respectively). As shown in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence intensity did not illustrate any significant change after the addition of other ions except copper ions (10 μM), which demonstrated high selectivity of the NH2-CDs to Cu2+ against other metal ions. Further addition of Cu2+ to the NH2-CDs solution containing other cations, the fluorescence intensity decreased immediately and greatly, which indicated that the coexisting metal ions possessed negligible interference effect to the detection of Cu2+ and further demonstrated the high specificity and selectivity to Cu2+.

To further prove that surface amino groups was a key most factor that leading fluorescence quenching, the bare CDs synthesized from citric acid and 2.0G-PAMAM was utilized to evaluate and study under the same ions conditions. As shown in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence intensity of bare CDs without amino modification illustrated no obvious quenching effect to other metal ions except Cu2+. The less than half fluorescence quenching effect to Cu2+ attributed to little surface amino group, which demonstrated that surface amino of CDs plays an important roles in the detection of Cu2+.

Furthermore, considering amino acids may interact with the NH2-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) and interfere the detection of Cu2+, some amino acids (including 10 μM Pro, ALa, Lys, Met, Ser, GLy, GLu, Leu, Val, Hyp, His, Cys and GSH) were also taken into account. As shown in Fig. 5C, most amino acids at the common concentration didn't illustrate any fluorescence quenching responses of NH2-CDs. However, the fluorescence intensity decreased immediately after addition of Cu2+ to the NH2-CDs solutions even in the presence of the above amino acids, which demonstrated that high selectivity of NH2-CDs to Cu2+ against both metal ions and amino acids. The negligible interference from other substances demonstrated the potential application of the NH2-CDs for detecting Cu2+ in biological and physiological environment.

The pH value of the NH2-CDs solution was a key factor that affects the sensing system. The initial fluorescence intensity (without Cu2+) and the quenched fluorescence intensity (with Cu2+) of the NH2-CDs are both pH-dependent (Fig. 5D). The quenched fluorescence intensity of NH2-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) was investigated at a wide pH ranged from 4 to 9. The addition of Cu2+ had almost no affection to the fluorescence intensity of the NH2-CDs in strongly acidic media (pH ≤ 6), which was attributed to the reason that the amino groups were protonated and unable to complex with Cu2+ to form cupric amine moieties. Similarly, it got significant influence in strongly alkaline solutions (pH = 9), which resulted from the fact that copper hydroxide was formed by Cu2+ and OH–. However, most physiological conditions (6 ≤ pH ≤ 8), the fluorescence intensity change of the NH2-CDs is preciously dependent on the concentration of Cu2+, which demonstrated the potential application of the NH2-CDs in clinical trials.
Photostability is one of the most significant factors that should be considered in developing fluorescent sensors.

Various buffer (including PBS, Tris-HCl, Tris-HAc, Tris-NaAc, Tris-EDTA and so on) in physiological condition were utilized to investigate the photostability of NH2-CDs (50 μg·mL−1). Fig. 6 A demonstrated no obvious interference to the fluorescence of NH2-CDs. Similar result was accept from different concentration of inorganic salt, which was represented by sodium chloride (Fig. 6B). These two experiments demonstrated NH2-CDs possessed strong photostability to overcome interference from different inorganic salt.

Photo-bleaching experiment was performed to examine the photostability of NH2-CDs (50 μg·mL−1). As shown in Fig. 6C, More than 95% of the fluorescence intensity of NH2-CDs remained after the NH2-CDs in aqueous solutions was continuously irradiated with a 400 W iodine–tungsten lamp at room temperature for about 2 h, which suggest that NH2-CDs a much high and strong photostability. Storage time also was utilized to evaluate the photostability of NH2-CDs. As shown in Fig. 6D, the fluorescence intensity of NH2-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) illustrated no obvious quench even in 8 weeks under dark and cold environment, which demonstrated NH2-CDs a suitable and reasonable fluorescence nanosensor with long-term stability.

These four experiments demonstrated NH2-CDs possessed high photostability and strong anti-interference ability in different condition, which is especially suitable for biological detection in complex condition.

To explore the possible application of the NH2-CDs in copper detection in vitro, MTT assay was carried out to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the NH2-CDs by HepG-2 and MCF-7 cells. As showed in Fig. 7 , the cell viability was higher than 95% over a concentration range from 2 to 100 μg·mL−1, which demonstrated that the obtained NH2-CDs with good cell permeability, low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility and great potential to monitor Cu2+ in living cells.

The confocal fluorescence cellular imaging experiment for detecting intracellular Cu2+ was performed. MCF-7 and HepG-2 cells were chosen to investigate the viability of the NH2-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) for intracellular Cu2+ imaging, which were captured under a laser-scanning confocal microscope in blue (410–490 nm) channel with excitation at 405 nm. MCF-7and HepG-2 cells were incubated with NH2-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) for 5 h at 37 °C, then treated with Cu2+ (250 nM, 10 μM and 100 μM) for 5 min. As shown in Fig. 8 , the fluorescence intensity of HepG-2 cells were found to be quite high and strong at the concentration of 50 μg·mL−1. The fluorescence of NH2-CDs in HepG-2 quenched quickly after treated with Cu2+ from fluorescence images picture (Fig. 8). The blue fluorescence decreased after addition of Cu2+ to cells and it decreased more greatly with copper concentration increased, which is identical to the fluorescence intensity results of the NH2-CDs to Cu2+ in PBS (Fig. 1D). The fluorescence results confirmed that the NH2-CDs responded Cu2+-dependent fluorescence imaging in cells, which demonstrated that the nanoprobe can be utilized for intracellular copper ions detection and imaging.

Similarly, the same experiment results and conclusion can be confirmed in MCF-7 cells (Fig. 8), which demonstrated that the NH2-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) was a common probe for Cu2+ detection in different cells and our sensor could be further expanded to intracellular copper ions detection and imaging for more complex biological samples.

Fluorescence response velocity was also investigated by HepG-2 and MCF-7cells incubated with 50 μg·mL−1 NH2-CDs for 2 h (Fig. 9 and Video 1). The quenching time was recorded by laser-scanning confocal microscope after addition of Cu2+ (100 μM). The confocal image illustrated that the fluorescence intensity decreased quickly with the time increased, while the fluorescence intensity became negligible in less than 33 s. The experimental result demonstrated that NH2-CDs was rapidly responsive and highly sensitive to Cu2+ over various biological interface.

For further confirm the obtained NH2-CDs was uptaken by cell, depth scaning was carried out after HepG-2 cells incubated with 50 μg·mL−1 NH2-CDs in complete medium for 5 h. As showed in Fig. 10 , confocal fluorescence images in Z axis scanned MCF-7 cells in various depth, the result indicated the fluorescence of NH2-CDs increased accompany with scanning height increasing from 150.76 to 144.76 μm. This phenomenon indicated that NH2-CDs deeply permeated into cellular membrane and entered into cells rather than adsorbed on the surface of cellular membrane. All these results consistently demonstrated that NH2-CDs good cell permeability and a potential universal application for Cu2+ detection in various live cells.

It is known that CDs are good electron donors and acceptors, amino-group is utilized to recognize Cu2+ with high selectivity [30–32]. As indicated by Fig. 4C and 8, the fluorescence intensity of the NH2-CDs decreases significantly with the increase of Cu2+ concentrations, while the fluorescence intensity of the bare CDs just decreases by half. Since the surface of CDs are covered with abundant amino groups (including primary amine, secondary amine, tertiary amine and amide) determined by FTIR (Fig. 1C) and XPS (Fig. 2). Because of large amount of ethylenediamine groups on the surface of NH2-CDs, it is highly possible that the amino groups bind Cu2+ to form cupric amine, resulting in a strong and selective fluorescence quenching of the NH2-CDs (Scheme 2). Cu2+ are wrapped and bonded by NH2-CDs to form dendritic cupric amine. It is a possible to promote an effective non-radiative energy and electron transfer from the photo-excited NH2-CDs to the cupric amine, resulting in quenching of NH2-CDs, which is highly consistent with the reported carbon dots for copper ions detection [33–35].

In summary, the amino-functionalized carbon dots (NH2-CDs) were designed and prepared from amine grafted on bare-CDs surfaces through ethylenediamine, which were demonstrated to be precise and selective nanosensor for Cu2+ detection. The amino groups at the surface of NH2-CDs can capture Cu2+ and formed an absorbent complex, resulting in the sensitive quenching fluorescence signal. After optimization of the experimental conditions, the fluorescence signal illustrated excellent linear relationship to copper ion concentration from 0.005 to 600 μM. The NH2-CDs illustrated numerous advantages, including strong sensitivity and photostability, high selectivity, rapid fluorescence response, neglected interference, good cell permeability and low cytotoxicity, wide linear detection range and simple preparation. The nanoprobe has been applied to image trace Cu2+ in MCF-7 and HepG-2 cells, which confirmed its on-site visual detection of Cu2 + in biological applications, suggested promising potential applications in disease early diagnosis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from Foundation of Shaanxi Provincial Education Department (16JK1770) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation on the 61st Project (2017M613190). Shaanxi Provincial Natural Science Basic Research Project, China (2018JM2037) and Opening Foundation of Key Laboratory of Resource Biology and Biotechnology in Western China (Northwest University), Ministry of Education (ZSK2018008).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105494.
